core_takeaway: Agent_1 set the consensus frame and kept Apple, but Agent_5 locked Stone as non-negotiable; Stone was worth 66 versus Apple at 44, so the last position outscored the first.
why_or_counterexample: Counterexample: the first agent's allocation frame largely won, but the last agent's top item was protected by the group and had a higher utility value.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened with an Apple-preserving baseline, then the group converged on a top-item allocation. Agent_5, speaking last, repeatedly made Stone non-negotiable and the others absorbed that as a shared constraint.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Available. The five private-thinking entries describe the same Stone-preserving/top-item allocation: A1 protects Apple, A5 protects Stone, and A2-A4 pursue Quill, Pencil, and Jewel.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Available. Agents 1-4 submitted the same final allocation that became the recorded final allocation. Agent_5 submitted a divergent two-agent allocation that removed Stone from itself and gave A3/A4 nothing. The public tabulation is internally inconsistent because it lists proposal #5 as 0 accept/5 reject but then says #5 was accepted; raw votes and final allocation support proposals #1-#4.
phase_notes.private_voting: Available. Raw private votes accept proposals #1-#4 and reject #5; reasons repeatedly cite top-item payoffs and Stone preservation.
phase_notes.reflection: Absent; the transcript contains no reflection entries.

mechanisms.theme: Consensus optimized top items rather than first-mover surplus
mechanisms.explanation: Once the shared target became A1 Apple, A2 Quill, A3 Pencil, A4 Jewel, A5 Stone, position order mattered less than matching each agent to its strongest item. That secured A1's target but also secured A5's higher-valued target.

evidence.quote: End-state: A1 Apple, A2 Quill, A3 Pencil, A4 Jewel, A5 Stone.
Proposed allocation aligns with the Stone-preserving end-state

mechanisms.theme: Agent_5 converted Stone into a group constraint
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_5's last-position message did not introduce a better global allocation, but it made Stone untouchable. Other agents accepted no Stone movement, which guaranteed A5 its 66-point item.

evidence.quote: Stone is with Agent_5 now and should stay there
I will not trade Stone away

mechanisms.theme: Agent_1 had agenda-setting power but only capped at Apple
mechanisms.explanation: The first speaker established a one-item baseline with Apple assigned to itself and later restated the consensus path. This helped A1 preserve its top item, but it did not give A1 more than the 44-point Apple payoff.

evidence.quote: highest-value item with me as my top priority
Agent_1 keeps Apple the whole time.

mechanisms.theme: Absolute payoff scale favored the last agent
mechanisms.explanation: Both first and last agents got their top item. The observed -22 gap comes from valuation magnitudes: A1's Apple is 44, while A5's Stone is 66.

mechanisms.theme: The late formal proposal failed even though the last agent won utility
mechanisms.explanation: The voting record does not support a simple last-mover proposal advantage. Proposal #1 received unanimous acceptance, while Agent_5's distinct proposal was rejected because it broke the Stone-preserving/top-item arrangement.
